name: principles
description: Declared statements — guiding rules or tenets, each as one terse sentence.
purpose: Use for design tenets, working agreements, or decision rules. Each principle is a single declarative statement; the layout renders it at display weight with a large counter as the visual anchor.

principles.description: One li per principle: a short declarative statement, authored as an ordered list (the counter renders as a large display numeral). No bold and no separate justification — keep each principle to one terse line.

skeleton:
<!-- _class: principles -->

## How we make calls when the spec is silent.

1. Default to the choice that is cheaper to reverse.
2. Document the decision, not the menu of options.
3. Reversible calls don't need a meeting.
sample:
<!-- _class: principles -->

## How we make calls when the spec is silent.

1. Default to the choice that is cheaper to reverse.
2. Name the actor, never the system.
3. Write the bet on the same slide as the choice.
4. Disagree in the room, then commit outside it.
5. Optimise for the reader who wasn't there.

whenToUse.title: Working agreements
whenToUse.body: Team tenets, design principles, decision rules. Use when the slide's job is to declare how the room makes calls, not to show data.

whenToUse.title: Stated as commands
whenToUse.body: Each principle is a short imperative sentence — "Default to X", "Name the actor". Statements with hedging language lose the principle's bite.

whenToUse.title: Three to six items
whenToUse.body: More than six principles and the audience stops remembering them. If you have more, prioritize: the principles slide carries the load-bearing few.

antiPatterns.title: Aspirations, not principles
antiPatterns.body: "Be empathetic" is a value, not a principle. Principles are decision rules — they say what to do when two options conflict. Reach for content if it's a values statement.

antiPatterns.title: Long per-principle prose
antiPatterns.body: Each principle is one terse sentence — there is no separate justification slot. If a principle needs a paragraph of context, give it its own slide and let principles act as the index.

antiPatterns.title: Hedged statements
antiPatterns.body: "We try to default to X" reads as a wish. Drop the hedge — principles are declared, not negotiated. "Default to X" lands harder.

related.name: actors
related.when: rules attach to named owners, not shared tenets

related.name: list
related.when: items are short bullets without title + body shape

related.name: cards-stack
related.when: each principle needs two sentences of body

numbered.label: Numbered
numbered.caption: The default counter — decimal-leading-zero (01, 02, 03). Author each principle as one plain declarative statement; the layout already sets display weight.
numbered.sample:
<!-- _class: principles -->

## How we resolve conflicts — top wins.

1. Default to the choice that is cheaper to reverse.
2. Name the actor, never the system.
3. Write the bet on the same slide as the choice.
4. Let form follow function.
5. Keep one main idea per slide.

lettered.label: Lettered
lettered.caption: Swaps the decimal counter for an A / B / C letter sequence. One plain statement per item. Use when the principles are referenced by letter elsewhere in the deck.
lettered.sample:
<!-- _class: principles lettered -->

## How we make calls when the spec is silent.

1. Default to the choice that is cheaper to reverse.
2. Name the actor, never the system.
3. Write the bet on the same slide as the choice.
4. Let form follow function.
5. Keep one main idea per slide.

roman.label: Roman
roman.caption: Numbers the principles with Roman numerals (I, II, III). Reads as a more formal canon — house rules, doctrine, a charter.
roman.sample:
<!-- _class: principles roman -->

## The editorial canon.

1. Plain words beat clever ones.
2. One claim per sentence.
3. Show the number, then the verdict.
4. Cut the adverb, keep the verb.
5. End on the decision, not the summary.

bullet.label: Bullet
bullet.caption: Replaces the numeric counter with a plain bullet. Use when the principles are a set with no rank or sequence — order carries no meaning.
bullet.sample:
<!-- _class: principles bullet -->

## What we optimise for, in no particular order.

1. Reversibility over consensus — make the cheap-to-undo call now.
2. Clarity over completeness — one true thing beats five hedged ones.
3. Ownership over process — processes don't get paged.
4. Evidence over instinct — write the prediction down so it can be scored.
